question: What happens when compiling: function getLength<T>(val: T) { return val.length }?

options.text: Error: Property 'length' does not exist on type 'T'
Code compiles, length will be undefined for types without length
options.explanation: TypeScript doesn't allow accessing properties that aren't guaranteed by the type. Without a constraint, T can be any type

options.text: Code compiles, TypeScript automatically adds a HasLength constraint
options.explanation: TypeScript never automatically adds constraints. You need to explicitly specify T extends { length: number }

options.text: Code compiles with a warning
options.explanation: TypeScript doesn't have warnings — only errors. Accessing a non-existent property is always an error

question: What type does getProperty(user, 'age') return if user: { name: string, age: number } and getProperty<T, K extends keyof T>(obj: T, key: K): T[K]?

options.text: number
string | number
options.explanation: T[K] is an indexed access type. When K = 'age', T['age'] = number. TypeScript doesn't widen the type to a union of all properties

options.text: unknown
options.explanation: T[K] exactly corresponds to the type of the specific property, not unknown. In this case K is narrowed to 'age'

options.text: string | number | undefined
options.explanation: TypeScript knows 'age' is an existing key (thanks to K extends keyof T), so undefined is not added

question: Which of the following generic type declarations are correct?

options.text: interface A<T, U = string> {}
interface B<T extends object, U extends keyof T = keyof T> {}
interface C<T = string, U> {}
options.explanation: Required (no default) parameters cannot follow optional (with default) parameters. U without a default comes after T with a default

options.text: interface D<T extends U, U = object> {}
options.explanation: Parameter T references U in its constraint, but U is declared later. While TS may allow this in some cases technically, it leads to unpredictable behavior

question: When should you NOT use a generic parameter?

options.text: When the parameter is used in only one position (only in argument or only in return)
When the function accepts an object with a known structure
options.explanation: A generic can be useful even for objects with known structure if you need to preserve the exact type (e.g., for a builder pattern)

options.text: When the function returns void
options.explanation: A void-returning function may still need a generic to relate the types of multiple parameters to each other

options.text: When the type can be replaced with any
options.explanation: Replacing a generic with any is an anti-pattern that loses type safety. If a generic isn't needed, use unknown instead

question: What does the construct new (...args: TArgs) => T do in a generic context?

options.text: Describes a constructor type that takes arguments of type TArgs and creates an instance of type T
Creates a new instance of function T with arguments TArgs
options.explanation: This is not a constructor call but a type description. 'new' in a type position means a constructor signature

options.text: Describes a regular function returning T
options.explanation: The 'new' keyword in a type distinguishes a constructor from a regular function. Without 'new' it would be a regular callable signature

options.text: Is syntactic sugar for typeof T
options.explanation: These are completely different constructs. typeof T returns the type of a value, while new () => T describes a constructor
